behaviors.description: Log entries with the same trace ID are grouped together regardless of which microservice emitted them.
behaviors.justification: Grouping logic is purely in-process: given a set of log entries, apply identity function on trace_id field and aggregate. No I/O, no concurrency, no real-time dependency. A real failure (e.g., grouping logic ignores service boundary or corrupts trace IDs) is caught by unit test with mock entries. Mock cannot miss this.
behaviors.test_description: Provide test entries with identical trace_id from different services, plus entries with distinct trace_ids. Assert all same-trace_id entries colocate in one group regardless of service origin. Test boundary: duplicate trace_id with 100+ entries, mixed service order, null/empty trace_id handling.

behaviors.description: The timeline view orders entries by timestamp, correctly handling clock skew of up to 500ms between services.
behaviors.justification: Clock-skew tolerance is a deterministic sorting/correction algorithm on (timestamp, service) tuples. Real failures (off-by-one in comparison, skew window applied incorrectly, entries still out of order after correction) are all caught by unit test with mock timestamps. No real time or I/O required.
behaviors.test_description: Create entries where service A's clock lags service B by 500ms, plus entries arriving out-of-order. Assert final timeline is ordered correctly after skew tolerance is applied. Test boundaries: clock skew exactly 500ms, one entry with skew far outside 500ms window, all entries from same service, single entry.

behaviors.description: A latency spike (p99 above 3x the baseline) is detected and flagged in the anomaly report.
behaviors.justification: Anomaly detection is deterministic: compute percentile on latency array, compare to threshold. Real failures (incorrect percentile calculation, wrong comparison operator, threshold hardcoded as 2x instead of 3x) are caught by unit test with mock latency distributions. No I/O or estimation required.
behaviors.test_description: Create two latency datasets: baseline (1000 entries, p99≈15ms) and spike (100 entries, p99≈100ms ≈ 6.7x baseline). Assert spike period is flagged as anomaly, baseline period is not. Test boundaries: p99 exactly 3x baseline (boundary condition), empty array, single latency value, p99 at 2.99x and 3.01x threshold.

behaviors.description: Malformed JSON log entries are skipped without crashing the pipeline, and the count of skipped entries is reported.
behaviors.justification: Error handling is testable at Unit via mocked input: provide malformed JSON strings to parser, assert no uncaught exception, assert skip count is correct. Real failures (parser crashes on invalid JSON, skip counter not incremented, pipeline gets stuck after error) are caught. Integration/System (real file I/O) could reveal encoding issues, but core error handling is deterministic.
behaviors.test_description: Pass a stream mixing valid JSON, syntactically invalid JSON (missing quotes, unmatched braces), and non-JSON text. Assert: (1) parser does not raise uncaught exception, (2) skip count matches the count of malformed entries, (3) all valid entries before and after malformed entries are processed, (4) no processing halt or deadlock occurs.

behaviors.description: The tool handles a burst of 10,000 log entries per second without dropping entries or falling behind.
behaviors.justification: Throughput under load is fundamentally a real-I/O test: in-memory mocks do not reveal bottlenecks in file reading, network I/O, or buffering. Real failure (entries dropped due to slow disk I/O, backpressure not handled, queue overflow) is hidden by Unit/Integration in-memory tests. Must measure against real I/O or a realistic simulator (e.g., message queue) to catch dropping and backlog.
behaviors.test_description: Load 10,000 log entries into a real file or stream. Invoke the tool and measure end-to-end latency. Assert: (1) count(processed) == 10,000 (no dropped entries), (2) latency < 2 seconds (headroom for 10k/sec burst), (3) no warnings about buffer overflow or dropped entries in logs, (4) memory footprint does not exceed a reasonable bound (no unbounded queue growth).
